content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Why the most brilliant reasoning engine is also the most forgetful.
Large Language Models represent a genuine breakthrough in pattern recognition.
Feed Claude 3.5 a fifty-page technical document, and it will summarize, critique, and refactor it with startling fluency. The output is coherent, reasoned, and often indistinguishable from what a senior engineer might produce.
And yet, ask that same model to maintain a complex codebase over a six-hour session — to recall why you chose a specific database index, or to remember that obscure environment variable you configured three days ago — and it will falter.
The model is not deficient in reasoning. It is structurally amnesiac.
This is the central paradox of applied generative AI: remarkable cognitive capacity coupled with a fragile, finite working memory known as the context window.
The LLM does not forget in the human sense. It never possessed the capacity to remember in the first place. Each interaction begins with a clean slate, constrained entirely by the text provided in that specific moment.
To transform an LLM from a clever chat interface into a persistent, reliable engineering partner, we cannot rely on the model alone. We must build an external architecture around it.
The Snapshot Problem
